title: Superconductor-"Metal" Transition of One-dimensional Interacting Bosons with Ohmic Quantum Dissipation
abstract: The phase diagram of a system of interacting bosons (Cooper pairs) hoping on a one-dimensional (1D) lattice with onsite phase dissipation describing the Josephson tunneling to a nearby diffusive normal-metal electrode is studied. Starting from the system at commensurate lattice filling, it is shown by a combination of analytical techniques that the phase diagram contains two quantum phases: A dissipative Bose-Einstein condensate (D-BEC) or superconductor with long-range phase coherence, and a dissipative Mott insulator (D-Mott) or "metal" with exponentially decaying phase correlations in space and local imaginary-time correlations decaying as the local pairing correlations of the electrode. The D-Mott/metal phase can be described as a 1D array of dissipative boson puddles, weakly coupled by Josephson tunneling. The puddle size roughly corresponds to the length scale beyond which phase slips suppress phase coherence. The dissipative time-dependent Ginsburg-Landau theory phenomenologically used by Sachdev, Werner, and Troyer [Phys. Rev. Lett. {\bf 92} 237003 (2004)] for the superconductor-metal transition in quasi-1D wires is derived from this microscopic puddle picture. Thus, the criticality of the D-Mott/D-BEC transition is shown to belong to the Wilson-Fisher universality class with dynamical exponent $z\approx 2$. At small doping, the D-Mott/metal phase remains stable due to its finite compressibility, which is computed to leading order in a perturbation expansion of the dissipation strength and the inter-puddle Josephson coupling. At larger doping, using a mapping to a pseudospin chain combined with bosonization, the D-BEC/superconductor phase is the ground state for non-vanishing but arbitrarily small dissipation. Similarities and differences with deconfinement transition of an array 1D bosonic Mott insulators in anisotropic optical lattices are also discussed.
summary: This paper theoretically studies the superconductor-metal transition in 1D interacting bosons coupled to an Ohmic bath. Using advanced techniques like bosonization and RG analysis, the authors map the system to an effective field theory, predicting the critical transition belongs to the Wilson-Fisher universality class. It provides a deep understanding of how dissipation stabilizes different quantum phases in low-dimensional quantum matter.
main_problem: The paper investigates the quantum phase transition between a superconducting state (D-BEC) and a metallic state (D-Mott) in one-dimensional interacting bosons subjected to Ohmic quantum dissipation.
main_result: The critical transition belongs to the Wilson-Fisher universality class with a dynamical exponent $z \approx 2$. The system exhibits distinct behaviors depending on the doping level and dissipation strength.
method: The analysis combines analytical techniques, including bosonization, renormalization group (RG) flow equations, and mapping the system to an effective nonlinear sigma model (NL$\sigma$M) or dissipative Ginzburg-Landau theory.
model_or_system: The model consists of interacting bosons (Cooper pairs) hopping on a 1D lattice, coupled to a dissipative bath via onsite phase dissipation (Josephson tunneling). The system is analyzed in the continuum limit as an array of weakly coupled bosonic puddles.
key_observables: Phase coherence (long-range vs. exponentially decaying), compressibility ($\chi$), and the correlation function $G_	heta(x, 	au)$.
important_parameters: Dissipation strength ($\alpha$), inter-puddle Josephson coupling ($J$), and doping level.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on perturbative expansions in the coupling constants and utilizes continuum approximations (e.g., deriving the NL$\sigma$M).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the phase diagram showing the boundary between the D-BEC and D-Mott phases, and show the decay of correlation functions in different regimes.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the microscopic Hamiltonian and action, through bosonization and RG analysis of the continuum limit, to deriving effective field theories like the dissipative TDGL theory, and finally calculating correlation functions in the ordered and disordered phases.
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed theoretical framework for understanding how dissipation fundamentally alters quantum phase transitions in low-dimensional systems, connecting microscopic models to universal critical behavior.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Phase diagram for the bosonized model (8) for a com- mensurate system (i.e. δ = 0) as a function of the Luttinger parameter, K, and the dimensionless dissipation strength, α. A comparison is shown between the approximate solu- tion (dashed line, obtained assuming a constant K) and the full numerical solution of the renormalization-group equations (10) to (12). The analytical curve is indicated by the dashed line, while the numerical solution is represented by the thick continuous line. The line α = 0 corresponds to a bosonic Mott insulator for K ≲2. For the numerical and analytical solutions the following values are assumed: gu(0) = 2 × 10−3

FIG. 2. Sketch of the D-Mott/Metal phase after coarse- graining the system: The 1D boson system + bath breaks into an array of weakly Josephson-coupled bosonic puddles that are coupled to individual Ohmic baths. The latter col- lectively represent a nearby diffusive metal electrode. The bosons (Cooper pairs of a 1D superconductor) can leak in and out of the puddles as indicated by the arrows. The char- acteristic size of the puddles ≈ξu is (roughly) determined by the length scale beyond which phase slips caused by the underlying lattice suppress coherent boson tunneling (see dis- cussion in Sec. V).

FIG. 3. Sketch of the D-Mott/Metal phase diagram as a func- tion of the chemical potential µ and the dissipation strength α; k is an integer. The boson-boson interactions are assumed to be short ranged, meaning that the Luttinger parameter K in Eq. (8) fulfills K > 1
